text: Death Valley National Park
Elsewhere in the Mojave, just north of Joshua
Tree, A seemingly endless noon grills a vast expanse of barren dunes.
Lying far below sea level, this is Death Valley, the most exotic of our national parks.
East of the Sierra Nevada, between the arid Great Basin and the Mojave,
this is the largest park in the contiguous United States.
Though there's often snow on the distant Paramount Mountains, it can be the hottest place in North America.
In August, a brutal 120 degrees Fahrenheit in the shade,
if shade can be found. Hot enough to melt a pair of shoes.
A sign at Badwater Basin marks the lowest spot on the continent.
A drop from Death Valley's highest mountain, 11 ,049 -foot telescope peak to Badwater Basin,
is twice the depth of Grand Canyon.
There were large lakes here once, in the prehistoric past.
But the Earth's climates changed, and the lakes dried up.
Yet, Death Valley exudes a strange beauty all its own.
As America's poet Mark Strand has discovered, its million -plus areas of
yellows, oranges, The orange, faint reds and purple smudges
of manganese suggest a Martian landscape, or what we believe the reaches of Mars to be like.
Precious water from rare desert springs sustains wildlife.
Humans were drawn here in the 1900s by the land's mineral wealth. Mines were constructed.
Borax, a mineral with many industrial uses, was extracted from the ancient salt crusts lining the lakes that had once shimmered here.
The borax was hauled to the railroad in huge 20 -mule team wagons.
Tent City sprang up which gave way to settlements of wood and stone
Rhyolite is one of them
Today, Rhyolite, just outside the park is a ghost town and well worth a visit
Now, these ghost towns bleach in the torrid sun
Until one day, they simply fade back
into the desert all isn't ghostly
After a lengthy stretch of bareness, of raw, harsh wasteland a visitor encounters this.
No, it isn't a mirage or a sign of sunstroke.
This architectural hybrid, part Spanish, part Mediterranean, part Hollywood back lot
is Scotty's castle the most famous man -made attraction in Death Valley
though he never really owned it it's named after Death Valley Scotty
the wildest two -legged critter ever to dwell in these parts
as a young cowboy Scotty travels with Buffalo Bill's Wild West show.
Performing as a trick rider and sharpshooter. But these aren't Scotty's only talents.
He has an inborn knack for, you might say, playing tricks with the truth.
Plain folks call it lying. One of his imaginary grub stakes is
a pie -in -the -sky gold mine in a little -known valley.
Well, Scotty goes east to Chicago to sell shares in his pipe dream mine to unsuspecting businessmen.
As luck would have it, he peddles a third of the shares to Albert Johnson, the wealthy president of the National Life Insurance Company.
Scotty had corralled a real sucker. Poor Johnson hadn't seen so much as a single gold nugget.
Pleased with himself, Scotty returns to Death Valley, leaving the swindled Albert Johnson behind in Chicago.
Or does he? A telegram reaches Scotty.
Albert Johnson is coming to Death Valley to see for himself this gold mine of which he is now part owner.
Scotty's alarmed, but can't this weigh Johnson from coming out?
En route to Death Valley, Albert Johnson and Scotty are halted by masked bandits.
It's a holdup. Rumor has it that Scotty has a holdup staged as a delaying tactic.
In any case, Johnson returns to Chicago, never having seen the bogus goldmine.
But Johnson makes another trip. Scotty's beside himself.
It seems that Johnson has some serious health problems and finds the desert air healing.
As a matter of fact, he hasn't felt so well in years as he does in Death Valley.
His visits become longer and more frequent and soon he's bringing his wife Bessie along with him.
Bessie too likes the desert, but she dislikes sleeping in a tent among rattlesnakes and scorpions.
Bessie wants something more comfortable. And so, in the 1920s,
construction begins on what comes to be called Scotty's Castle.
Today you can tour the opulently furnished rooms, the great hall,
and the music room with its theater organ of 1 ,121 pipes.
The Johnsons allow their mansion to be called Scotty's Castle.
The three of them become fast friends and Albert Johnson lets it be known that the castle's construction
was financed with profits from the gold mine, which never did exist.
Though touring Scotty's Castle is a stellar attraction, Death Valley offers sundry other activities
of the kind that tone the body. For the rugged, during the cooler months,
hiking is popular and being the Wild West, horseback riding
one can make Death Valley a golfing vacation with a whole new take on golf
the 18 hole par 70 furnace Creek course is the world's lowest elevation golf course
there's a noticeable difference in how the ball response. This course is a challenge.
Seasonally the stubbornly dry desert yields to brief torrents of winter rain.
And the desert explodes
in a springtime array of exotic flowers.
This is Death Valley when it is most alive.
